burial.plot_notes: Weston Cemetery, Rensselaer, Jasper County Indiana.

parents.father_research_notes: Dr. John Ritchey, native of Pennsylvania, practiced medicine. Married Mary Cooney. 11 children: James, Jane, Samuel W., Powers, John, Lucinda, Clarinda, Lawrence P., Louisa, Andrew J., Sarah. Family moved from Erie County PA to Butler County OH when James was age 9 (~1813). Not built.

education.notes: Studied medicine at Ohio Medical College Cincinnati. Reared + educated Butler County OH before medical training.

career.notes: Located in Franklin Johnson County IN 1829 (age 25) and practiced medicine there for 36 years until 1865 relocation.
1865 purchased 400 acres in Jasper County and relocated there for the last 23 years of his life.

career.organization: Indiana General Assembly, House of Representatives
career.notes: Elected 1840 as Democrat from Johnson County. Two years in House. Chairman of Educational Affairs Committee. First to make a report favorable to the Insane Hospital at Indianapolis.
Three years in Indiana Senate (specific years to be researched). Continued chairmanship of Educational Affairs.
Delegate to the Indiana Constitutional Convention of 1850-1851 — the body that drafted the second Indiana state constitution still substantially in force.
1848 presidential elector for Lewis Cass (Democratic candidate). Stumped the state for Cass.
Delegate to three National Conventions: 1844 (Polk Democrat), 1856 (Fremont Republican), 1860 (Lincoln Republican). Political evolution from Democrat to Liberal Republican over his career.

narrative_blocks.body: Served as a delegate to the 1851 Indiana Constitutional Convention — the body that drafted the second Indiana state constitution still substantially in force today. Two years in the Indiana House of Representatives plus three years in the Senate, chairing the Educational Affairs Committee.

narrative_blocks.header: Delegate to Polk, Fremont, and Lincoln nominations
narrative_blocks.body: Delegate to three National Conventions across different parties: 1844 Polk Democrats, 1856 Fremont Republicans, and 1860 Lincoln Republicans. Also a Lewis Cass elector in 1848 who 'stumped the state' for the Democratic ticket. Politically evolved from Democrat to Liberal Republican.

narrative_blocks.header: First favorable report on Indianapolis Insane Hospital
narrative_blocks.body: As Chairman of Educational Affairs in the Indiana House 1840-42, he 'was the first to make a report favorable to the Insane Hospital at Indianapolis' — the institution now known as Central State Hospital, Indiana's first state mental-health facility (opened 1848).

burial.plot_notes: Greenlawn Cemetery, Franklin, Johnson County Indiana — separate from her Huntington family at Center Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age thirty-five leaving four young children
narrative_blocks.body: Died June 2, 1849 at age thirty-five — leaving four surviving children Emily, Angelina, Mary Louisa, and Clarinda, per the 1888 contemporary history of her husband. Two other children (Augustus 1839-1843 and James Lawrence 1844-1844) had died young before her. Her husband Dr. James Ritchey survived her by nearly forty years and would marry twice more.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — pioneer-era family cemetery; same cemetery as wife Mary Tyler Morse Huntington (I01146) and son Henry Augustus Huntington (TD0263).

career.notes: Per Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea.' Specific maritime career details not documented.

Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). 'A man honored by his fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence' per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record. Died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed 1819 from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana
narrative_blocks.body: Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana in 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). His six children all born between 1811 and 1825 followed him west; he died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Spent some portion of his life at sea
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea' before settling in Indiana as a respected citizen. Honored by fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence in his character.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside husband Septimus.

parents.father_research_notes: Morse family of Wrentham (Norfolk County) MA. Wrentham was the same town as the Morses (Samuel F.B. Morse the telegraph inventor's family). Specific parents not yet identified.
